title: My Mother at Sixty-six by Kamala Das
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Ageing, separation, fear of loss, and the bond between mother and daughter. The poem shows how we hide pain behind smiles when facing mortality.

notes.heading: Key Literary Devices
points: Simile: “like that of a corpse”, “pale as a late winter’s moon”
Contrast: young trees and children versus the old mother
Repetition: “smile and smile and smile” shows forced reassurance

summary:
This poem captures the poet’s fear of losing her ageing mother and the pain of separation. Kamala Das is driving to the Cochin airport with her mother, who is sixty-six years old. When she looks at her mother dozing beside her, her face looks pale and ashen, like a corpse. This sudden realization of her mother’s old age and mortality brings back a childhood fear: the fear of losing her mother.

To distract herself, the poet looks out of the car window. She sees young trees sprinting past and happy children spilling out of their homes. The trees and children symbolize life, energy, and youth, the opposite of her mother’s frailty. After the security check at the airport, she looks at her mother again. Her mother’s face is “wan, pale as a late winter’s moon.” This simile shows her mother’s dull and fading appearance and suggests loneliness and the approach of death.

The poet feels the old familiar ache of childhood, the fear that her mother will die. But she hides her feelings because she does not want to show her mother how upset she is. So she smiles and says, “See you soon, Amma.” This cheerful goodbye hides her deep sorrow and fear. The repeated smile is forced and is meant to comfort both her mother and herself, even though she knows separation and ageing are inevitable.
